perturbation.original_text: NPC shall process all data received by it reflecting the Visa and MasterCard sales transactions and any related return credits by the Merchants and shall submit to the applicable Visa or MasterCard interchange networks ("Interchange") "Settlement Files" reflecting such transactions and directing the applicable Interchange to pay the net amount due to an account established by NCB for the purpose of receiving all settlement amounts paid by Visa and MasterCard with respect to the transactions processed by NPC pursuant to this Agreement and effecting appropriate payments to the Merchants and NPC in accordance with this Agreement ("the NCB Account").
perturbation.changed_text: NPC shall process all data received by it reflecting the Visa and MasterCard sales transactions and any related return credits by the Merchants and shall submit to the applicable Visa or MasterCard interchange networks ("Interchange") "Transaction Records" reflecting such transactions and directing the applicable Interchange to pay the net amount due to an account established by NCB for the purpose of receiving all settlement amounts paid by Visa and MasterCard with respect to the transactions processed by NPC pursuant to this Agreement and effecting appropriate payments to the Merchants and NPC in accordance with this Agreement ("the NCB Account").
perturbation.explanation: Changing “Settlement Files” to “Transaction Records” introduces a legal contradiction because 'Settlement Files' refers to a specific data set and process with particular legal meanings in financial regulations. 'Transaction Records' is a more generic term that can be misinterpreted.
perturbation.contradicted_law: This contradicts regulations related to the reporting of suspicious activity under the Bank Secrecy Act (BSA), which requires precise reporting of financial transactions including settlement data.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Bank Secrecy Act (BSA) requires financial institutions to maintain accurate records and report suspicious activities. By changing 'Settlement Files' to 'Transaction Records', the contract potentially obscures the specific data required for BSA compliance (31 CFR § 1020.320), making it harder to detect and report suspicious transactions.

perturbation.justification: The change from “Settlement Files” to “Transaction Records” likely creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that the Bank Secrecy Act (BSA) requires maintaining accurate records and reporting suspicious activities (31 CFR § 1020.320). The Perturbation Explanation notes that 'Settlement Files' has a specific legal meaning in financial regulations, while 'Transaction Records' is a more generic term. The LLM Law Explanation claims this obscures data required for BSA compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is directly relevant, mentioning 31 CFR 1020.320, which pertains to suspicious transaction reporting. While this snippet is limited, the Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation, and the change in terminology support the claim that the modified contract potentially hinders compliance with BSA regulations, thus creating a contradiction, falling under the definition of Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction: contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions.

perturbation.original_text: NPC agrees to comply with all laws and regulations relating to confidentiality of customer lists and other information which are applicable to NCB and its agents or to NPC. NCB agrees to comply with all contractual obligations of NPC actually known to NCB and all laws and regulations applicable to NCB or NPC relating to confidentiality of customer lists and other information.
perturbation.changed_text: NPC agrees to comply with all general business practices relating to confidentiality of customer lists and other information. NCB agrees to comply with all contractual obligations of NPC actually known to NCB and all general business practices applicable to NCB or NPC relating to confidentiality of customer lists and other information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing “laws and regulations” with “general business practices” weakens the obligation to comply with legally mandated confidentiality requirements, potentially conflicting with privacy laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: This directly contradicts the requirements for safeguarding customer information under the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (GLBA).
perturbation.law_explanation: The GLBA (15 U.S.C. § 6801) mandates specific safeguards for customer financial information. Replacing 'laws and regulations' with 'general business practices' weakens the legal obligation and could be interpreted as non-compliance with GLBA's mandated security and confidentiality measures, such as implementing a written information security program.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 94 / SUBCHAPTER I / § 6801 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 6801 : Protection of nonpublic personal information Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 94-PRIVACY SUBCHAPTER I-DISCLOSURE OF NONPUBLIC PERSONAL INFORMATION Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §6801. Protection of nonpublic personal information (a) Privacy obligation policy It is the policy of the Congress that each financial institution has an affirmative and continuing obligation to respect the privacy of its customers and to protect the security and confidentiality of those customers' nonpublic personal information. (b) Financial institutions safeguards In furtherance

Are you up on what the revised Rule requires? FTC's Privacy Rule and Auto Dealers: FAQs Auto dealers that extend credit, arrange financing or leasing, or give financial advice must notify customers about the information they collect, who they share it with, and how they protect it. Are you following the rules of the road? How To Comply with the Privacy of Consumer Financial Information Rule of the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Financial institutions covered by the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act must tell their customers about their information-sharing practices and explain to customers their right to "opt out" if they don't want their information shared with certain third parties. Is your company following the requirements of the Privacy Rule? perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to comply with legal requirements by replacing "laws and regulations" with "general business practices." This change directly contradicts the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (GLBA), which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, addresses concerns relating to consumer financial privacy and requires financial institutions to maintain safeguards to protect the security of customer information. Furthermore, Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 U.S.C. § 6801, emphasizes the 'affirmative and continuing obligation' of financial institutions to protect the privacy and confidentiality of customers' nonpublic personal information. The shift to 'general business practices' undermines this legally mandated obligation, creating a potential legal contradiction as it suggests a lower standard of compliance than what the law requires, potentially failing legal scrutiny and triggering a legal violation.
